title: FinanceBench: A New Benchmark for Financial Question Answering
corpus_composition: 10,231 hand-curated questions over real public-company filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, earnings reports) of large US public companies. Each question has a grounded answer plus supporting passage citations. Includes 'refuse if not in context' cases.
input_representation: Long-document QA. Questions reference specific filings; full filings (often 100k+ tokens) are the source of truth.
core_mechanism: Open-ended QA over real recent filings, with explicit support for retrieval-augmented evaluation. Questions are constructed to require multi-hop reasoning over financial statements (matching line items across periods, computing derived figures, locating disclosures across exhibits). Refusal-when-unsupported is part of the eval rubric.
key_empirical_claim: Strong general LLMs (GPT-4-class) reach only ~20-30% correctness under realistic open-book retrieval settings. The benchmark exposes a large capability gap between current models and useful financial QA. Closed-book performance is near-random — the test is fundamentally about retrieval + reasoning + refusal, not memorization.
benchmark_and_metrics: Pass-rate (correct / incorrect / refuse), with human-grade rubrics for correctness given citation. Open-book + closed-book reported.
ablation_or_evidence_notes: Retrieval baselines (BM25, embedding-based) and several closed/open model baselines reported. Closed-book vs open-book ablation is a clean demonstration that the task is retrieval-bound. Limited public data on how varying retriever quality moves headline numbers across models.
foundation_model_potential: Not a model. But it defines what counts as deployable financial competence on the long-context-filings side: grounded answers to real filings under realistic retrieval noise.
why_relevant_for_inhouse_training: Single best public benchmark to optimize an in-house financial FM against on the filings side, exactly because closed-book is uninterpretable — it forces evaluation of the full pipeline (retrieval + reasoning + refusal). Every training iteration should be measured here, with retrieval held fixed.
main_limitations: US-only, large-cap-only, English-only. Finite question set — overfitting risk if used as both train and eval. Questions cluster on a few statement types.
deployment_risks: Contamination (since release, public 10-Ks have been ingested by many pretraining corpora — open-book is robust to this but closed-book numbers leak); benchmark exhaustion (small enough that targeted training overfits); regime narrow (large-cap US only); evaluator drift (human-graded rubric).
implementation_takeaways: 1) Canonical eval for the long-context-and-retrieval route. 2) Always run with retrieval; closed-book is uninterpretable. 3) Hold out 10% of questions as a contamination-free split kept out of any training mixture. 4) Fix the retriever when comparing models; vary it as a separate ablation.
must_test_hypotheses: H_fb1: A strong RAG pipeline over a vanilla strong general Instruct model outperforms fine-tuned 7B finance models on FinanceBench by >=10pp. If true, retrieval > SFT for long-context filings QA. H_fb2: Filings-specific continued-pretraining helps FinanceBench only in combination with retrieval — closed-book lift is not worth the compute.
open_questions: Has anyone re-run FinanceBench with a fixed-retrieval protocol across multiple base models, so numbers are actually comparable? Most reporting uses different retrieval configs.
